title: Chirality-free photon routing via giant atoms in waveguide QED ladders
authors: Glenn Ochsner, Vincent Schena, Arushi Deb, Imran M. Mirza
abstract: In this paper, we present an in-depth examination of single-photon routing in a multi-emitter waveguide quantum electrodynamics ladder with up to five giant atoms simultaneously coupled across two linear waveguides. Using a real-space approach, we analyze a non-chiral routing architecture and evaluate the impact of scaling the number of giant atoms in three topologically distinct configurations: fully separated, simply braided, and fully nested. Using numerical results, we show that increasing the number of giant atoms, in separate or braided configurations, greatly expands the operational parameter space for near-deterministic ($\sim$100%) photon routing into the target waveguide, achieving directionality through quantum interference alone. In contrast, the nested architecture is limited to a maximum routing efficiency of 25% due to rigid geometric symmetries, although this limitation could be mitigated by employing less symmetric coupling distances. Finally, we assess the system under direct interatomic interactions and environmental dissipation. Our results show that, while spontaneous emission reduces transport probabilities, the multi-atom-separated configuration maintains high routing efficiencies across broad operational windows of incoming photon frequency without requiring chiral coupling.
summary: This paper theoretically investigates single-photon routing in quantum electrodynamics ladders built with arrays of giant atoms. By analyzing different atomic arrangements—separated, braided, and nested—the authors demonstrate that the braided geometry offers superior, wideband, and robust routing capabilities. The results show that increasing the number of atoms enhances performance and provides resilience against environmental loss.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of achieving highly efficient, deterministic, and chirality-free single-photon routing in complex quantum optical circuits.
main_result: The braided topology of multiple giant atoms proves to be the most robust architecture, enabling near-perfect routing even when environmental dissipation is present.
method: The analysis employs a real-space quantum optics approach, solving the time-independent Schrödinger equation in the single-photon excitation sector using techniques derived from the Bethe Ansatz.
model_or_system: The system models up to five 'giant atoms' coupled to two linear waveguides, forming various topological arrangements (separated, braided, nested). The dynamics are governed by a Hamiltonian incorporating atomic transitions, waveguide propagation, and coupling strengths.
key_observables: Photon routing efficiency ($T_q$ or $|t_2|^2$), which quantifies the probability of a photon entering the target waveguide.
important_parameters: Detuning ($\Delta$), phase accumulation ($	heta$), coupling rates ($\Gamma$), and the environmental loss parameter ($\gamma/\Gamma$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to the single-photon excitation subspace, and the initial models assume specific coupling geometries (e.g., coupling at exactly two points for single-atom analysis).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic setups for the three topologies (separated, braided, nested) for $n$ atoms, and plot the routing probability $|t_2|^2$ as a function of detuning and phase, both in the lossless and lossy limits.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from single-atom analysis to two-atom systems, systematically comparing the performance of the three topologies. It then scales up to $n$ atoms, investigating the impact of direct interatomic interactions (all-to-all vs. nearest-neighbor) and environmental dissipation ($\gamma$).
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and quantum optics as it uses engineered quantum systems (giant atoms) to control single-photon quantum information flow, moving beyond simple cavity QED setups into complex waveguide arrays.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (Color online) Single photon router using a single giant atom, coupled to each waveguide at x1 = 0 and x2 = d. The photon enters the setup from the bottom left.

FIG. 2: (Color online) Photon transmission and reflection probabilities in waveguides 1 and 2 for a single giant atom. Detuning ∆is varied from −30 to +30 in units of the atom-waveguide coupling rate Γ. The phase θ, which incorporates the separation d between coupling points, is varied from 0 to 2π.

FIG. 3: (Color online) A single photon router using two giant atoms, coupled to a top and bottom waveguide at points x1, x2, x3, and x4 with coupling strength parameters VI1, VI2, VII1, and VII2 in a (a) fully separated, (b) simply braided, and (c) fully nested configuration.

FIG. 4: (Color online) The routing efficiency |t2|2 as a function of the normalized detuning ∆/Γ and the accumulated phase θ, assuming identical atoms (∆I = ∆II = ∆), uniform decay rates (Γpql = Γ), and vanishing direct coupling (g/Γ = 0). For the separate and nested configurations, the coupling points are equally spaced such that the phase accumulation between any two adjacent coupling points is given by θ. For the braided configuration, the phase accumulation is non-uniform, satisfying 2θ = k(x2 −x1) = k(x4 −x3) and θ = k(x3 −x2).

FIG. 5: (Color online) Routing efficiency |t2|2 is shown as a function of normalized detuning ∆/Γ and accumulated phase θ for a non-zero direct interaction strength g/Γ = 3. All other parameters and coupling configurations are identical to those in Fig. 4.
